perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and interpreted and enforced in accordance with, the laws in force in the country of Korea excluding any conflict of laws rule or principle which might refer such construction to the laws of another jurisdiction). Each party hereto irrevocably submits to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Korea with respect to any matter arising hereunder or related hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and interpreted and enforced at the sole discretion of E-Smart Technologies, Inc. Each party hereto submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of New York with respect to any matter arising hereunder or related hereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to favor one party and remove any reference to Korean law creates an imbalance and could violate Korean regulations regarding fair contract terms. This can make it more difficult for Lee & Pak to enforce their rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Act on the Regulation of Terms and Conditions (Korea)
perturbation.law_citation: Article 6 (1) of the Act on the Regulation of Terms and Conditions
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 (1) of the Act on the Regulation of Terms and Conditions prohibits unfair contract terms that significantly disadvantage one party. Specifying that the agreement will be governed at the sole discretion of E-Smart Technologies, Inc and changing jurisdiction to the courts of New York could be deemed unfair to Lee & Pak, potentially violating this law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces Korean law with the sole discretion of E-Smart Technologies, Inc. and changes jurisdiction to New York courts. The Perturbation Explanation indicates this creates an imbalance and violates fair contract terms under Korean regulations. The Law Explanation specifically mentions Article 6 (1) of the Act on the Regulation of Terms and Conditions prohibiting unfair contract terms that significantly disadvantage one party. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Snippet 2 is just a law search interface, the explanations alone suggest that removing Korean law and jurisdiction in favor of one party could be deemed unfair and violate Korean law, making the change a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may Disclose Confidential Information if required by law, provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior written notice of the requirement and provide reasonable assistance to enable the Disclosing Party to seek protection of its Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may Disclose Confidential Information if required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior written notice and assistance to the Disclosing Party if Confidential Information is required to be disclosed by law removes a crucial protection for the Disclosing Party and could violate Korean trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Unfair Competition Prevention and Trade Secret Protection Act (Korea)
perturbation.law_citation: Article 2 (2) of the Unfair Competition Prevention and Trade Secret Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 2 (2) defines trade secrets and provides protection for them. By removing the requirement for prior notice and assistance, the Receiving Party could disclose trade secrets more easily, potentially violating the Disclosing Party's rights under this act. The original clause provides a procedural safeguard; its removal weakens the protection of confidential information and potentially enables unfair competition.

perturbation.justification: The original text required the Receiving Party to provide prior written notice and assistance to the Disclosing Party before disclosing confidential information as required by law. The changed text removes this requirement. The Perturbation Explanation states this removes a crucial protection for the Disclosing Party and could violate Korean trade secret law. The Law Explanation says that by removing this requirement, the Receiving Party could disclose trade secrets more easily, potentially violating the Disclosing Party's rights. The scraped snippets do not contain any helpful information, but the change creates a contradiction because it removes a procedural safeguard that weakens the protection of confidential information. This constitutes an omission of a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant as per the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The parties at all times, and from time to time, and upon every reasonable written request to do so, shall make, do, execute, deliver or cause to be made, done, executed and delivered all such further acts, deeds, assurances, and things as may be required for more effectually implementing and carrying out the true intent and meaning of this Agreement. Each party agrees to act in the utmost good faith of a level required by a trustee in the implementation of the obligations required hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties shall make reasonable efforts to implement the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the obligation to 'reasonable efforts' instead of 'utmost good faith of a level required by a trustee' significantly weakens the commitment to implementing the agreement. This could violate the principle of good faith in Korean contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 2 of the Civil Act states the necessity of acting in good faith (신뢰성실의 원칙). By changing the commitment from acting in utmost good faith to making only 'reasonable efforts,' the modified text potentially contradicts this fundamental principle of Korean law, making it easier for a party to avoid their obligations under the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'utmost good faith of a level required by a trustee,' whereas the changed text only requires 'reasonable efforts.' The Law Explanation states this potentially contradicts the principle of acting in good faith under Article 2 of the Civil Act. Since the modification significantly reduces the level of commitment required, this constitutes a direct weakening of a core legal obligation, suggesting a contradiction. The Omission Legal Contradiction definition also aligns as the original commitment is omitted.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that, notwithstanding the execution of the Agreement, each Disclosing Party maintains the sole and absolute discretion to determine what, if any, of its Confidential Information shall be disclosed to the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that, notwithstanding the execution of the Agreement, E-Smart maintains the sole and absolute discretion to determine what, if any, of its Confidential Information shall be disclosed to Lee&Pak.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the text to give 'sole and absolute discretion' only to E-Smart regarding what confidential information is disclosed creates a significantly unbalanced agreement and could be seen as an abuse of bargaining power. This would violate the principle of fairness in contract law in Korea.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Act on Fair Transactions in Subcontracting (Korea)
perturbation.law_citation: Article 4 of the Act on Fair Transactions in Subcontracting
perturbation.law_explanation: While technically concerning subcontracting relationships, Article 4 prohibits abuse of superior bargaining position. Concentrating discretionary power in E-Smart's hands only and removing Lee&Pak's equivalent right makes the agreement unfair, suggesting E-Smart has abused a stronger negotiating position, violating the spirit of the law, especially if Lee & Pak can be seen as acting in a dependent role.

perturbation.justification: The original text provides discretion to both parties regarding the disclosure of confidential information, while the changed text grants sole discretion to only E-Smart. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this could be seen as an abuse of bargaining power and a violation of fairness. The Law Explanation suggests that Article 4 (though technically about subcontracting) prohibits abuse of superior bargaining position. Concentrating discretionary power only in E-Smart could be seen as unfair, suggesting E-Smart has abused a stronger negotiating position, violating the spirit of the law.
